html:
Canto III

The talk of Vasco da Gama with the King of Melinde, wherein he describeth Europe, and recounteth the origin of the kingdom of Portugal, its kings (including the King Dom Fernando) and its principal achievements: The notable feat of Eg√°s Mon√≠z: The Queen of Castile, Dona Maria, visiteth Portugal to crave aid for the Battle of the Salado: The loves and luckless fate of Dona Ign√©z de Castro: Some events which befell the King Dom Fernando.

Populous Europe passeth in review; (6‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝ23)
Eg√°s Mon√≠z is praised for famous feat; (35‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝ41)
What Kings and wars our Lusitania knew; (24‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝ34)
Afonso sees the Christ on Cross elate; (42‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝ46)
Agnes de Castro‚Äôs breast of snowy hue (118‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝ135)
With innocent blood empurpleth popular hate:
Fernando‚Äôs vile neglect is shown to shame, (138‚ÅÝ‚Äì‚ÅÝ143)
And the high pow‚Äôers that youth and beauty claim.

1

Now, my Calliope! to teach incline
what speech great Gama for the King did frame:
Inspire immortal song, grant voice divine
unto this mortal who so loves thy name.
Thus may the God whose gift was Medicine,
to whom thou barest Orpheus, lovely Dame!
never for Daphne, Clytia, Leucoth√∂e
due love deny thee or inconstant grow he.

2

Satisfy, Nymph! desires that in me teem,
to sing the merits of thy Lusians brave;
so worlds shall see and say that Tagus-stream
rolls Aganippe‚Äôs liquor. Leave, I crave,
leave flow‚Äôry Pindus-head; e‚Äôen now I deem
Apollo bathes me in that sovran wave;
else must I hold it, that thy gentle sprite,
fears thy dear Orpheus fade through me from sight.

3

All stood with open ears in long array
to hear what mighty Gama mote unfold;
when, past in thoughtful mood a brief delay,
began he thus with brow high-raised and bold:‚ÅÝ‚Äî
‚ÄúThou biddest me, O King! to say my say
anent our grand genealogy of old:
Thou bidd‚Äôst me not relate an alien story;
thou bidd‚Äôst me laud my brother Lusians‚Äô glory.

4

‚ÄúThat one praise others‚Äô exploits and renown
is honour‚Äôd custom which we all desire;
yet fear I ‚Äôtis unfit to praise mine own;
lest praise, like this suspect, no trust inspire;
nor may I hope to make all matters known
for Time however long were short: yet, sire!
as thou commandest all is owed to thee;
maugre my will I speak and brief will be.

5

‚ÄúNay, more, what most obligeth me, in fine,
is that no leasing in my tale may dwell;
for of such Feats whatever boast be mine,
when most is told, remaineth much to tell:
But that due order wait on the design,
e‚Äôen as desirest thou to learn full well,
the wide-spread Cont‚Äôinent first I‚Äôll briefly trace,
then the fierce bloody wars that waged my Race.

6

‚ÄúAtwixt the Zone, where Cancer holds command‚ÅÝ‚Äî
the lucent Sun‚Äôs septentrional mete‚ÅÝ‚Äî
and that whose frigid horrors freeze the land
as burns the middle Belt with fervid heat,
lies haughty Europe: On her goodly strand,
facing Arcturus and the Ponent, beat
the briny billows of Atlantis plain,
while free t‚Äôwards Auster flows the Midland-main.

7

‚ÄúThat part where lovely Dawn is born and bred,
neighboureth Asia: But the curv√®d river,41
from far and frore Rhipaean ranges shed,
to feed Maeotis-lake with waves that shiver,
departs them, and the Sea-strait fierce and dread,
that owned the vict‚Äôory of the Greek deceiver,
where now the seaman sees along the shore
triumphant Troja‚Äôs mem‚Äôories and‚ÅÝ‚Äîno more.

8

‚ÄúThere farther still the Boreal Pole below,
Hyperbor√©an mountain-walls appear,
and the wild hills where Aeolus loves to blow,
while of his winds the names they proudly bear:
Here such cold comfort doth Apollo show,
so weak his light and warmth to shine and cheer,
that snows eternal gleam upon the mountains,
freezeth the sea, and ever freeze the fountains.

9

‚ÄúHere of the Scythic hordes vast numbers be,
in olden day a mighty warrior band,
who fought for honours of antiquity
with the then owners of the Nylus-land:
But how remote their claims from verity,
(for human judgments oft misunderstand),
let him who seeks what higher lore reveal‚Äôd
ask the red clay that clothes Damascus-field.42

10

‚ÄúNow in these wild and wayward parts be told
Cold Lapland‚Äôs name, uncultivate Norw√Ýy,
Escandinavia‚Äôs isle, whose scions bold
boast triumphs Italy shall ne‚Äôer gainsay.
Here, while ne frost, ne wintry rigours hold
in hand the waters, seafolk ply the way,
over the arm of rough Sarmatic Main
the Swede, the Brusian,43 and the shiv‚Äôering Dane.

11

‚ÄúBetween the sea and Tana√Øs-stream we count
strange races, Ruthens,44 Moscows, and Livonians‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Sarm√°tae all of old‚ÅÝ‚Äîand on the Mount
Hercynian,45 Marcomanni, now Polonians.
Holding the empire Almayne paramount
dwell Saxons, and Bohemians, and Pannonians;
and other tribes, wherethrough their currents frore
Rhine, Danube, Amasis,46 and Albis pour.

12

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄôTwixt distant Ister and the famous Strait,
where hapless Helle left her name and life,
the Thracians wone, a folk of brave estate,
Mars‚Äô well-loved country, chosen home of strife:
There Rh√≥dope and Haemus rue the weight
of curs√®d Othman‚Äôs rule with horror rife;
Byzance they hold beneath their yoke indign
great injury working to great Constantine!

13

‚ÄúHard by their side the Macedonians rest,
whose soil is water‚Äôed by cold Axius‚Äô wave:47
Eke ye, of ev‚Äôery choicest realm the best,
Lands of the free, the wise, the good, the brave,
that here did breed and bear the facund breast,
and to the world its wit and wisdom gave,
wherewith thou, noble Greece! hast reach‚Äôed the stars,
no less by arts exalt than arms and wars.

14

‚ÄúThe Dalmats follow; and upon the Bay
where rose Antenor‚Äôs walls48 in while of yore,
superb Venetia builds on wat‚Äôery way,
Adria‚Äôs Queen that erst was lowly poor.
Hence seawards runs a land-arm made to sway
forceful the sons of many a stranger shore;
an arm of might, whose Race hath conquer‚Äôd time
nor less by spirit than by sword sublime.

15

‚ÄúGirdeth her shores the kingdom Neptunine,
while Nature‚Äôs bulwarks fence her landward side;
her middle width departeth Apennine,
by Mars, her saint and patron, glorified:
But when the Porter rose to rank divine,
she lost her prowess, and her bellic pride:
Humbled she lies with antique puissance spent:
So Man‚Äôs humil‚Äôity may his God content!

16

‚ÄúGallia can there be seen, whose name hath flown
where Caesar‚Äôs triumphs to the world are told;
by S√©quana49 ‚Äôtis watered and the Rhone,
by Rhine‚Äôs deep current and Garumna50 cold:
Here rise the ranges from Pyr√©ne known,
the Nymph ensepulchre‚Äôd in days of old,
whence, legends say, the conflagrated woods
rolled golden streams, and flow√®d silvern floods.

17

‚ÄúLo! here her presence showeth noble Spain,
of Europe‚Äôs body corporal the head;
o‚Äôer whose home-rule, and glorious foreign reign,
the fatal Wheel so many a whirl hath made:
Yet ne‚Äôer her Past or force or fraud shall stain,
nor restless Fortune shall her name degrade;
no bonds her bellic offspring bind so tight
but it shall burst them with its force of sprite.

18

‚ÄúThere, facing Tingitania‚Äôs shore, she seemeth
to block and bar the Med‚Äôiterranean wave,
where the known Strait its name ennobled deemeth
by the last labour of the Theban Brave.
Big with the burden of her tribes she teemeth,
circled by whelming waves that rage and rave;
all noble races of such valiant breast,
that each may justly boast itself the best.

19

‚ÄúHers the Tarragonese who, famed in war,
made aye-perturbed Parthenop√© obey;
the twain Asturias, and the haught Navarre
twin Christian bulwarks on the Muslim way:
Hers the Gallego canny, and the rare
Castilian, whom his star raised high to sway
Spain as her saviour, and his seign‚Äôiory feel
Baetis, Leon, Gran√°da, and Castile.

20

‚ÄúSee the head-crowning coronet is she,
of general Europe, Lusitania‚Äôs reign,
where endeth land and where beginneth sea,
and Phoebus sinks to rest upon the main.
Willed her the Heavens with all-just decree
by wars to mar th‚Äô ignoble Mauritan,
to cast him from herself: nor there consent
he rule in peace the Fiery Continent.

21

‚ÄúThis is my happy land, my home, my pride;
where, if the Heav‚Äôens but grant the pray‚Äôer I pray
for glad return and every risk defied,
there may my life-light fail and fade away.
This was the Lusitania, name applied
by Lusus or by Lysa, sons, they say,
of ancient Bacchus, or his boon compeers,
eke the first dwellers of her eldest years.

22

‚ÄúHere sprang the Shepherd,51 in whose name we see
forecast of virile might, of virtuous meed;
whose fame no force shall ever hold in fee,
since fame of mighty Rome ne‚Äôer did the deed.
This, by light Heaven‚Äôs volatile decree,
that ancient Scyther, who devours his seed,
made puissant pow‚Äôer in many a part to claim,
assuming regal rank; and thus it came:‚ÅÝ‚Äî

23

‚ÄúA King there was in Spain, Afonso hight,
who waged such warfare with the Saracen,
that by his ‚Äôsanguined arms, and arts, and might,
he spoiled the lands and lives of many men.
When from Herc√πlean Calp√® winged her flight
his fame to Caucasus Mount and Caspian glen,
many a Knight, who noblesse coveteth,
comes off‚Äôering service to such King and Death.

24

‚ÄúAnd with intrinsic love inflam√®d more
for the True Faith, than honours popular,
they troop√®d gath‚Äôering from each distant shore,
leaving their dear-loved homes and lands afar.
When with high feats of force against the Moor
they proved of sing‚Äôular worth in Holy War,
will√®d Afonso that their mighty deeds
commens‚Äôurate gifts command and equal meeds.

25

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄôMid them Henrique, second son, men say,
of a Hungarian King, well-known and tried,
by sort won Portugal which, in his day,
ne priz√®d was ne had fit cause for pride:
His strong affection stronger to display
the Spanish King decreed a princely bride,
his only child, Theresa, to the count;
and with her made him Seigneur Paramount.

26

‚ÄúThis doughty Vassal from that servile horde,
Hagar the handmaid‚Äôs seed, great vict‚Äôories won;
reft the broad lands adjacent with his sword
and did whatever Brav‚Äôery bade be done:
Him, for his exploits exc‚Äôellent to reward,
God gave in shortest space a gallant son,
whose arm to ‚Äônoble and enfame was fain
the warlike name of Lusitania‚Äôs reign.

27

‚ÄúOnce more at home this conqu‚Äôering Henry stood
who sacred Hierosol‚Äôyma had reliev√®d,
his eyes had fed on Jordan‚Äôs holy flood,
which the Dear Body of Lord God had lav√®d;
when Godfrey52 left no foe to be subdued,
and all Judaea conquered was and sav√®d,
many that in his wars had done devoir
to their own lordships took the way once more.

28

‚ÄúBut when this stout and gallant Hun attain√®d
Life‚Äôs fatal period, age and travail-spent,
he gave, by Death‚Äôs necessity constrain√®d,
his sprite to Him who had that spirit lent:
A son of tender years alone remain√®d,
to whom the Sire bequeath‚Äôd his ‚Äôbodiment;
with bravest braves the youth was formed to cope,
for from such sire such son the world may hope.

29

‚ÄúYet old Report, I know not what its weight,
(for on such antique tale no man relies),53
saith that the Mother, tane in tow the state,
a second nuptial bed did not despise:
Her orphan son to disinher‚Äôited fate
she doomed, declaring hers the dignities,
not his, with seigniory o‚Äôer all the land,
her spousal dowry by her sire‚Äôs command.

30

‚ÄúNow Prince Afonso (who such style had tane
in pious mem‚Äôory of his Grandsire‚Äôs name)
seeing no part and portion in his reign
all pilled and plunder‚Äôd by the Spouse and Dame,
by dour and doughty Mars inflamed amain,
privily plots his heritage to claim:
He weighs the causes in his own conceit
till firm Resolve its fit effect shall greet.

31

‚ÄúOf Guimara‚Äôens the field already flow‚Äôd
with floods of civil warfare‚Äôs bloody tide,
where she, who little of the Mother show‚Äôd,
to her own bowels love and land denied.
Fronting the child in fight the parent stood;
nor saw her depth of sin that soul of pride
against her God, against maternal love:
Her sensual passion rose all pow‚Äôer above.

32

‚ÄúO magical Medea! O Progne dire!
if your own babes in vengeance dared ye kill
for alien crimes, and injuries of the sire,
look ye, Teresa‚Äôs deed was darker still.
Foul greed of gain, incontinent desire,
were the main causes of such bitter ill:
Scylla her ag√®d sire for one did slay,
for both Teresa did her son betray.

33

‚ÄúRight soon that noble Prince clear vict‚Äôory won
from his harsh Mother and her Fere indign;
in briefest time the land obeyed the son,
though first to fight him did the folk incline.
But reft of reason and by rage undone
he bound the Mother in the biting chain:
Eftsoons avenged her griefs the hand of God:
Such veneration is to parents owe‚Äôd.

34

‚ÄúLo! the superb Castilian ‚Äôgins prepare
his pow‚Äôer to ‚Äôvenge Teresa‚Äôs injuries,
against the Lusian land in men so rare,
whereon ne toil ne trouble heavy lies.
Their breasts the cruel battle grandly dare,
aid the good cause angelic Potencies;
unrecking might unequal still they strive,
nay, more, their dreadful foe to flight they drive.54

35

‚ÄúPasseth no tedious time, before the great
Prince a dure Siege in Guimaraens dree‚Äôd
by passing pow‚Äôer, for to ‚Äômend his state,
came the fell en‚Äôemy, full of grief and greed:
But when committed life to direful Fate,
Egas, the faithful guardian, he was free‚Äôd,
who had in any other way been lost,
all unprepar√®d ‚Äôgainst such ‚Äôwhelming host.

36

‚ÄúBut when the loyal Vassal well hath known
how weak his Monarch‚Äôs arm to front such fight,
sans order wending to the Spanish fone,
his Sovran‚Äôs homage he doth pledge and plight.
Straight from the horrid siege th‚Äô invader flown
trusteth the word and honour of the Knight,
Egas Moniz: But now the noble breast
of the brave Youth disdaineth strange behest.

37

‚ÄúAlready came the plighted time and tide,
when the Castilian Don stood dight to see,
before his pow‚Äôer the Prince bend low his pride,
yielding the promis√®d obediency.
Eg√°s who views his knightly word belied,
while still Castile believes him true to be,
sweet life resolveth to the winds to throw,
nor live with foulest taint of faithless vow.

38

‚ÄúHe with his children and his wife departeth
to keep his promise with a faith immense;
unshod and stripp√®d,55 while their plight imparteth
far more of pity than of vengeance:
‚ÄòIf, mighty Monarch! still thy spirit smarteth
to wreak revenge on my rash confidence,‚Äô
quoth he, ‚Äòbehold! I come with life to save
my pledge, my knightly Honour‚Äôs word I gave.‚Äô

39

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòI bring, thou seest here, lives innocent,
of wife, of sinless children dight to die;
if breasts of gen‚Äôerous mould and excellent
accept such weaklings‚Äô woeful destiny.
Thou se‚Äôest these hands, this tongue inconsequent,
hereon alone the fierce exper‚Äôiment try
of torments, death, and doom that pass in full
Sinis or e‚Äôen Perillus‚Äô brazen bull.‚Äô

40

‚ÄúAs shrifted wight the hangman stands before,
in life still draining bitter draught of death,
lays throat on block, and of all hope forlore,
expects the blighting blow with bated breath:
So, in the Prince‚Äôs presence angry sore,
Eg√°s stood firm to keep his plighted faith:
When the King, marv‚Äôelling at such wondrous truth,
feels anger melt and merge in Royal ruth.

41

‚ÄúOh the great Portingall-fidelity
of Vassal self-devote to doom so dread!
What did the Persian more for loyalty
whose gallant hand his face and nostrils shred?
When great Darius mourned so grievously
that he a thousand times deep-sighing said,
far he prefer‚Äôd his Z√≥p‚Äôyrus sound again,
than lord of twenty Babylons to reign.

42

‚ÄúBut Prince Afonso now prepared his band
of happy Lusians proud to front the foes,
those haughty Moors that held the glorious land
yon side where clear delicious Tagus flows:
Now on Ourique56 field was pitched and plan‚Äôd
the Royal ‚ÄôCampment fierce and bellicose,
facing the hostile host of Sarrasin
though there so many, here so few there bin.

43

‚ÄúConfident, yet would he in nought confide,
save in his God that holds of Heav‚Äôen the throne;
so few baptiz√®d stood their King beside,
there were an hundred Moors for every one:
Judge any sober judgment, and decide
‚Äôtwas deed of rashness or by brav‚Äôery done
to fall on forces whose exceeding might
a cent‚Äôury show√®d to a single Knight.

44

‚ÄúOrder five Moorish Kings the hostile host
of whom Ism√°r,57 so called, command doth claim;
all of long Warfare large experience boast,
wherein may mortals win immortal fame:
And gallant dames the Knights they love the most
‚Äôcompany, like that brave and beauteous Dame,
who to beleaguer‚Äôd Troy such aidance gave
with woman-troops that drained Therm√≤don‚Äôs wave.

45

‚ÄúThe coolth serene, and early morning‚Äôs pride,
now paled the sparkling stars about the Pole,
when Mary‚Äôs Son appearing crucified
in vision, strengthened King Afonso‚Äôs soul.
But he, adoring such appearance, cried
fired with a frenzied faith beyond control:
‚ÄòTo th‚Äô Infidel, O Lord! to th‚Äô Infidel:58
Not, Lord, to me who know Thy pow‚Äôer so well.‚Äô

46

‚ÄúSuch gracious marvel in such manner sent
‚Äôflam√®d the Lusians‚Äô spirits fierce and high,
towards their nat‚Äôural King, that excellent
Prince, unto whom love-boon none could deny:
Aligned to front the foeman prepotent,
they shouted res‚Äôonant slogan to the sky,
and fierce the ‚Äôlarum rose, ‚ÄòReal, real,
for high Afonso, King of Portugal!‚Äô

47

‚ÄúAs to the fight by calls defied and cries,
some fierce Molossan on the wooded height,
attacks the rampant Bull, who most relies
on strength of tem‚Äôerous horn to force the fight:
Now nips the ear, then at the side he flies
barking, with more of nimbleness than might,
till ripped at last the gullet of his foe
he lays the mighty bulk of monster low:

48

‚ÄúSo the new King, inflamed with zeal devout
for God nor less for faithful Lieges‚Äô sake,
assails by cunning skill the barb‚Äôarous rout
with Braves the fronting phalanx eath to break:
Whereat the bandogs ‚ÄòAllah! Allah!‚Äô shout,
and fly to arms; our raging warriors shake
the lance and bow; resound the trumpet tones;
the music thunders; Echo moans and groans.

49

‚ÄúE‚Äôen as the prairie-fire enkindled on
sun-parch√®d steppe (as winn‚Äôoweth upper air
sibilant Boreas), by the blasts swift blown
o‚Äôer bush and arid brake rains flame and flare:
The shepherd lads and lasses, idly strown
in rest and gentle slumber, waked by blare
of crackling conflagration blazing higher,
hamlet-wards force their flocks to fly the fire:

50

‚ÄúTh‚Äô astonied Moorman in such startled guise,
snatcheth his weapon hast‚Äôily and sans heed;
yet he awaits the fight, nor ever flies,
nay, spurs his battle-ginnet to its speed.
Meet him as rash and swift his enemies
whose piercing lances gar his bosom bleed:
These fall half-slain, while others flee that can
convoking aidance of their Alcoran.

51

‚ÄúThere may be view√®d ‚Äôcounters madly rash,
onsets no Serra‚Äôs sturdy strength could stand,
while charging here and there the chargers dash‚ÅÝ‚Äî
the gifts of Neptune smiting gravid Land:‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Buffets they deal, and blows that bash and smash,
burneth and blazeth Warfare‚Äôs blasting brand,
but he of Lusus coat, mail, plate of steel,
hacks, hews, breaks, batters, rives and rends piecemeal.

52

‚ÄúMen‚Äôs heads like bullets dance the bloody plain,
ownerless arms and legs insens‚Äôible lie,
and quiv‚Äôering entrails tell of mortal pain,
and faces fade and life‚Äôs fair colours fly.
Lost is that impious host, whose heap√®d slain
roll o‚Äôer the green‚Äôery rills of crimson dye;
whereby the grasses lose their white and green
and nought but glow of crimson gore is seen.

53

‚ÄúBut now the Lusian victor held the field
his trophies gath‚Äôering, and his gorgeous prey:
The crusht Hispanian Moor was forced to yield
while on the plain three days the great King lay.59
And now he chargeth on his virgin shield,
what still assures this well-won Vict‚Äôory.
five noble inescutcheons azure-hued,
signing the Moorish Five his sword subdued.

54

‚ÄúHe paints with bezants five each ‚Äôscutcheon,
the thirty silvers wherewith God was sold,
and various tinctures make His mem‚Äôory known,
whose grace and favour did his cause uphold.
Painted on every cinque a cinque is shown;
and, that the thirty may be fully told,
counteth for two the one that central lies
of the five azures painted crossy-wise.

55

‚ÄúSome time has pass√®d, since this gain had past
of goodly battail, when the high King hies
to take Leir√≠a, lately tane and last
conquest that boast our conquer‚Äôd enemies.
Herewith Arronches castled strong and fast
is jointly gain√®d with the noble prize
Scalabicastro,60 whose fair fields amene
thou, crystal Tagus! bathest all serene.

56

‚ÄúUnto this conquered roll of towns his might
eke addeth Mafra won in shortest space,
and in the Mountains which the Moon hath hight
he clasps frore Cintra to his hard embrace;
Cintra, whose Naiads love to hide their light
by hidden founts and fly the honey‚Äôd lace,
which Love hath woven ‚Äômid the hills where flow
the waters flaming with a living lowe.

57

‚ÄúAnd thou, O noble Lisbon! thou encrown‚Äôd
Princess elect of Cities capital,
rear‚Äôd by the facund Rover-King renown‚Äôd,
whose wiles laid low Dardania‚Äôs burning wall:
Thou, whose commands oblige the Sea‚Äôs Profound,
wast taught to bear the Lusitanian‚Äôs thrall,
aided by potent navies at what time
they came crusading from the Boreal clime.61

58

‚ÄúBeyond Germanic Albis and the Rhene,
and from Britannia‚Äôs misty margin sent,
to waste and slay the people Sarracene,
many had sailed on holy thoughts intent.
Now gained the Tagus-mouth, our stream amene
to great Afonso‚Äôs royal camp they went,
whose lofty fame did thence the Heav‚Äôens invade
and siege to Ulyss√©a‚Äôs walls they laid.

59

‚ÄúFive sequent times her front had Luna veil√®d,
five times her lovely face in full had shown,
when oped her gate the City, which avail√®d
no Force ‚Äôgainst ‚Äôsieging forces round her thrown.
Right bloody was th‚Äô assault and fierce th‚Äô assail√®d,
e‚Äôen as their stubborn purpose bound them down;
asp‚Äôerous the Victor, ready all to dare,
the Vanquisht, victims of a dire despair.

60

‚ÄúThus won she yielded and, in fine, she lay
prostrate that City which, in days of old,
the mighty meiny never would obey
of frigid Scythia‚Äôs hordes immanely bold:
Who could so far extend their savage sway,
till Ebro saw ‚Äôt, and Tagus trembling roll‚Äôd;
and some o‚Äôer Baetis-land, in short, so swept
that was the region Vandal√≠a ‚Äôclept.

61

‚ÄúWhat might of city could perchance endure
prowess which proud Lisb√≥a might not bear?
Who mote resist the powers dure and dour
of men, whose Fame from earth invadeth air?
Now yield obedience all Estremadure,
Obidos, Torres Vedras, Alemquer,
where softly plash the music-murmuring waves,
‚Äômid rocks and reefs whose feet the torrent laves.

62

‚ÄúEke ye, Transtagan lands! ye justly vain
of flavous Ceres‚Äô bien and bonny boon,
yielded to might above the might of men
the walls and castles by his valour won:
Thou, too, Moor-yeoman! hopest hope insane,
those riant regions long as lord to own;
for Elvas, Moura, Serpa, well-known sites,
with Alcacer-do-Sal must yield their rights.

63

‚ÄúThe noble City and sure seat behold,
held by Sertorius, rebel famed whil√≤me;
where now the nitid silv‚Äôery waters cold,
brought from afar to bless the land and home,
o‚Äôerflow the royal arches hundredfold,
whose noble sequence streaks the dark-blue dome;
not less succumb‚Äôd she to her bold pursuer,
to Girald√≥, entitled ‚ÄòKnight Sans Peur.‚Äô62

64

‚ÄúFast towards Beja city, vengeful prest,
to slake his wrath for spoilt Trancoso‚Äôs wrong,63
Afonso, who despiseth gentle rest
and would brief human life by Fame prolong.
Feebly resisteth him and his behest
the City, falling to his arms ere long,
and nought of life within her walls but feel
the raging victor‚Äôs edge of merciless steel.

65

‚ÄúWith these Palmella yielded to the war,
piscous Cezimbra, eke, her finny spoils;
then, aided onwards by his fortunate star,
the King a pow‚Äôerful force of foemen foils:
Felt it the City, saw ‚Äôt her Lord afar,
who to support and aid her spares no toils,
along the hill-skirt marching all unware
of rash encounter lackt he heed and care.

66

‚ÄúThe King of Bad‚Äôajoz was a Muslim bold,
with horse four thousand, fierce and furious Knights,
and countless Peons, armed and dight with gold,
whose polisht surface glanceth lustrous light.
But as a savage Bull on lonely wold,
whom jealous rage in hot May-month incites,
sighting a stranger, mad with love and wrath
the brute blind lover chargeth down the path:

67

‚ÄúSo doth Afonso, sudden seen the foes
that urge their forward march securely brave,
strike, slay, and scatter, raining doughty blows;
flies the Moor King, who recks but self to save:
Naught save a panick fear his spirit knows;
his foll‚Äôowers eke to follow only crave;
while ours, who struck a stroke so sore, so fell,
were sixty horsemen told in fullest tale.

68

‚ÄúVictory swift pursuing, rest disdaineth
the great untiring King; he must‚Äôereth all
the lieges of his land, whom nought restraineth
from ever seeking stranger realms to ‚Äôthrall.
He wends to ‚Äôleaguer Bad‚Äôajoz, where he gaineth
his soul‚Äôs desire, and battleth at her fall
with force so fierce, and art and heart so true
his deeds made others fain to dare and do.

69

‚ÄúBut the high Godhead, who when man offends,
so long deserv√®d penalties delays,
waiting at times to see him make amends,
or for deep myst‚Äôery hid from man‚Äôs dull gaze;
if He our valiant King till now defends
from dangers, fac√®d fast as foes can raise;
lends aid no longer, when for vengeance cries
the Mother‚Äôs curses who in prison lies;

70

‚ÄúFor in the City which he compast round,
encompast by the Leoneze was he,
because his conquests trespasst on their ground,
which of Leon and not of Port‚Äôugale be.
Here was his stubborn will right costly found,
as happeth oft in human history,
an iron maims his legs, as rage-inflam√®d
to fight he flies and falls a captive maim√®d.

71

‚ÄúO famous Pompey! feel thy Wraith no pain
to see the fate of noble feats like thine;
nor mourn if all-just Nemesis ordain
thy bays be torn by sire-in-law indign;
though Phasis frore and parcht Sy√©n√©-plain
whose perpendic‚Äôular shadows ne‚Äôer decline,
Bootes‚Äô ice-bergs, and Equator-fires,
confess the terrors which thy name inspires;

72

‚ÄúThough rich Arabia, and the brood ferocious
Heniochs, with Colchis-region known of yore
for Golden Fleece; and though the Cappadoces
and J√∫deans who One only God adore;
though soft Soph√©nes,64 and the race atrocious,
Cilician, with Armenia whence outpour
the twain of mighty streams, whose farthest fount
hides in a higher and a holier Mount;65

73

‚ÄúAnd though, in fine, from far Atlantic tide
E‚Äôen to the Taurus, Scythia‚Äôs tow‚Äôering wall,
all saw thee conquer; fearless still abide
if none save Emath-field beheld thee fall:
Thou shalt behold Afonso‚Äôs ovant pride,
lie subjugate, that subjugated all.
Such fate Celestial Counsel long foresaw
thine from a sire, his from a son-in-law.

74

‚ÄúReturned the King sublime, in fine, with sprite
by the just doom of Judge divine chastis√®d,
and when of Santarem in pride of might
the Saracen a bootless siege devis√®d;
and when of Vincent, martyr benedight,
the precious Corse by Christian people priz√®d,
from Sacrum Promontorium66 was conveyed
and reverent-wise in Ulyss√©a laid:

75

‚ÄúFaster to push the projects still in hand,
the toil-spent Father sent his stout young son,
bidding him pass to Alemtejo‚Äôs land,
with warlike gear and soldiers many a one.
Sancho, a sov‚Äôereign wielder of the brand,
straight forward passing, gore-red gars to run
the stream67 whose waters feed Seville and flood,
dyed by the brutish Moormen‚Äôs barb‚Äôrous blood.

76

‚ÄúWith hunger whetted by this new success,
now resteth not the Youth till sight his eyes
another slaughter, sore as this, oppress
the barb‚Äôrous host that circling Beja lies:
Not long the Prince whom fortune loves to bless,
waits the fair end where leads his dear emprize.
But now the routed Moors to vengeance cleave,
their only hope such losses to retrieve.

77

‚ÄúThey crowd the mighty Mount whereof Meduse
robb√®d his body who the skies upbore:
They flock in thousands from Cape Ampeluse
and from Tangier, Antaeus‚Äô seat of yore.
Abyla‚Äôs68 dweller offereth scant excuse;
who with his weapon hasteth him the more,
when heard the Moorish clarions shrilly-ton√®d,
and all the reign high Juba whilom own√®d.

78


‚ÄúThe Mir-almuminin,69 who led the throng,
from the Dark Cont‚Äôinent past to Portugal:
Thirteen Moor kings he led, high, haught, and strong,
to his Imperial sceptre subject all:
Thus wreaking forceful every tyrant Wrong,
wherever easy Wrong mote sate his gall,
Sancho in Santarem he flies t‚Äô invest,
yet his was hardly of success the best.

79

‚ÄúGives asp‚Äôerous battle, fighting fury-fraught
the hateful Moor a thousand feints designing;
ne horrid catapult avails him aught,
ne forceful batt‚Äôering-ram, ne hidden mining:
Afonso‚Äôs son, conserving force and thought,
and firm resolve with warlike skill combining,
foreseeth all with prudent heart and art,
and stern resistance brings to every part.

80

‚ÄúBut now the Vet‚Äôran‚ÅÝ‚Äîdoomed by years to ease
and gentle rest, from life of toil and teen,
be‚Äôing in the city,70 down whose pastured leas
Mondego‚Äôs wavelets kiss the hem of green;‚ÅÝ‚Äî
when learnt how close his son beleaguer‚Äôd is
in Santarem by Moormen blind with spleen,
close from the City flies the fone to meet,
age-idlesse spurning with fast eager feet.

81

‚ÄúHe heads his army, tried in war and known,
his son to succour; and his well-led host
shows wonted Port‚Äôingall-fury all their own,
till in brief time the Moor is broke and lost.
The Battle-plain‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhose blood-stained front is strown
with steely coats, and caps of varied cost,

heaped with their owners‚Äô mangled corpses lies.

82

‚ÄúForth fares the remnant of the Paynimry
from Lusitania, hurled in headlong flight:
But Mir-almuminin may never flee,
for ere he flee his life hath fled the light.
To Him whose arm vouchsafed such Victory
in praise and stintless prayer our hosts unite:
Chances so passing strange make clear to ken
God‚Äôs arm smites sorer than all arms of men.

83

‚ÄúSuch crown of conquest still bedeckt the brow
of old Afonso, Lord of lofty fame;
when he, in fine, who ever foiled his foe,
was foiled by ancient Time‚Äôs unyielding claim:
Past the death-sickness o‚Äôer his pallid brow
its frigid hand, and wrung his feeble frame;
and thus the debt on mortal shoulders laid
his years to gloomy Libit√≠na paid.

84

‚ÄúHis loss the lofty Promontories mourn,
and from the wavy rivers floods of grief
with lakelets overspread the field√®d corn,
and trickling tears are sorrow‚Äôs sole relief:
But ring so loud o‚Äôer Earth‚Äôs extremest bourne
the fame and exploits of our great lost chief,
that evermore shall Echo for his reign
‚ÄòAfonso! Afonso!‚Äô cry, and cry in vain.

85

‚ÄúSancho, his lusty son, the worthy heir
of his great Father‚Äôs valour, force and might,
as did his early doings clear declare,
when Baetis71 fled ensanguin‚Äôd from the fight,
and from Andalus√≠a forced to fare
the barb‚Äôarous King and peoples Ishmaelite;
and more, when they who vainly Beja girt
of his shrewd blows themselves had borne the hurt:

86

‚ÄúAfter he had been raised to Royal hest,
and held for years a few his father‚Äôs land,
he wends the city Sylves to invest,
plow√®d whose plain the barb‚Äôarous peasant‚Äôs hand:
With allies val‚Äôorous was his daring blest
the sturdy Germans, whose Armada man‚Äôd
by furnisht host was flying o‚Äôer the wave,
the lost Judaea to regain and save.

87

‚ÄúTo join in holy enterprise they went
Red Frederick, who did first to move begin
his mighty armament and succour sent
to ward the town where Christ had died for men;
When Guy, whose Croisers were by thirst bespent,
yielded his sword to gallant Saladin
there, where the Muslim host was well supplied
with wat‚Äôery store to those of Guy denied.72

88

‚ÄúBut that majestical Armade that came
by dint of storm-wind o‚Äôer the Lisbon bar,
would aid our Sancho the foul foe to tame,
all being bounden for the Holy War:
As to his Father, happed to him the same;
and Lisbon fell to fortunes similar;
aided by Germans, Sylves town he takes
and the fierce dweller slays or subject makes.

89

‚ÄúAnd if so many trophies from Mahound
his valour snatch√®d, eke denies his pride
the Leoneze in peace to till their ground,
whom Mart with martial usage loved to guide:
Till on the bended neck his yoke he bound
of haughty T√∫i and all its country-side;
where many a city felt the dreaded blow
which with thine arms thou, Sancho! broughtest low.

90

‚ÄúBut ‚Äômid his many palms this Prince waylaid
the stroke of tem‚Äôerous Death; his heir prefer‚Äôd
was that esteem√®d son whom all obey‚Äôd,
second Afonso, of our Kings the third.
He reigning, Alcac√©r-do-Sal was made
ours, snatcht for ever from the Moorish herd;
that erst was taken by the Moor beset,
and now parforce he pays of Death the Debt.

91

‚ÄúAfonso dying, straight to him succeedeth
a second Sancho, easy-going soul,
who in his weakling idlesse so exceedeth,
the rul√®d rule their ruler and their tool:
He lost the Reign, for which another pleadeth,
by private preference deprived of rule;
since, govern‚Äôd only by his minions‚Äô will
he made him partner in their works of ill.

92

‚ÄúYet ne‚Äôer was Sancho, no, such profl‚Äôigate pest
as was that Nero wedded with a boy,
who in foul incest showing horrid zest
his mother Agrippina dared enjoy;
Ne‚Äôer with strange cruel arts did he molest
the liege, nor gar‚Äôd the torch his town destroy;
he was no waster, no Heliogab√°lus,73
no woman-king like soft Sardanap√°lus.

93

‚ÄúNe‚Äôer was his tyr‚Äôannised people so chastis√®d
as wretched Sicill by her tyrant bane;
ne like the despot Ph√°laris, he devis√®d
novel inventions for inhuman pain:
But his high-hearted realm, which ever priz√®d
lords of the highest hopes and sovran strain,
would ne‚Äôer whole-soul√®d such a King obey,
who showed not fittest for the kingly sway.

94

‚ÄúHence came the gov‚Äôernance of the reign to right
the County Bolognese; and he arose
at length to kingship, when from life took flight
his brother Sancho sunk in soft repose.
This, whom the ‚ÄòBrave Afonso‚Äô subjects hight,
when fenced his kingdom from internal foes,
strives to dilate it; what his Sire possest
is worlds too narrow for so big a breast.

95

‚ÄúOf both Algarves, given to his hand
in gift of bridal dowry, greater part
his arm recovers, and outdrives the band
of Moors ill-treated now by hostile Mart.
He freed and made the Mistress of her Land
our Lusitania, such his bellic art;
till final ruin whelmed the mighty hordes
where‚Äôer Earth own√®d Lusus‚Äô subjects lords.

96

‚ÄúSee, next that Diniz comes in whom is seen
the ‚Äòbrave Afonso‚Äôs‚Äô offspring true and digne;
whereby the mighty boast obscur√®d been,
the vaunt of lib‚Äôeral Alexander‚Äôs line:
Beneath his sceptre blooms the land serene
(already compast golden Peace divine)
with constitution, customs, laws and rights,
a tranquil country‚Äôs best and brightest lights.

97

‚ÄúThe first was he who made Coimbra own
Pallas-Minerva‚Äôs gen‚Äôerous exercise;
he called the Muses‚Äô choir from Helicon
to tread the lea that by Mondego lies:
Whate‚Äôer of good whilere hath Athens done,
here proud Apollo keepeth ev‚Äôery prize:
Here gives he garlands wove with golden ray,
with perfumed Nard and ever-verdant Bay.

98

‚ÄúBrave towns and cities reared his hand anew,
stout fortalice, and strongly-castled mure,
while his well-nigh reform√®d kingdom grew
with stalwart towers and lofty walls secure:
But when dure Atropos cut short the clew,
and shore the thin-spun thread of life mature,
arose, to filial duty nidering
the fourth Afonso, yet a brave good King.

99

‚ÄúThis proud Castile‚Äôs bravades with equal pride
despised, of soul and breast serenely grand;
for aye the Lusitanian‚Äôs sprite defied
fear of the strongest, though the smaller band:
But when the Mauritanian races hied
to win and wear Hesperia‚Äôs winsome land,
and march√®d boldly to debel Castile
superb Afonso went to work her weal.

100

‚ÄúNe‚Äôer did Semiramis such myriads see
who o‚Äôer the wide Hydaspick prairie trod;
nor Attila‚ÅÝ‚Äîwho daunteth Italy
with dreadful boast, self-titled ‚ÄòScourge of God,‚Äô‚ÅÝ‚Äî
hurried such Gothick hosts to victory,
as the wild Saracens‚Äô stupendous crowd,
with all th‚Äô excessive might Gran√°da yields
that flockt to battle on Tartessus‚Äô fields.

101

‚ÄúWhen saw Castilia‚Äôs monarch, high and haught,
such force inexpugnable fain of strife,
dreading lest all Hispania come to naught,
once lost ere this,74 far more than loss of life;
aid of our Lusian chivalry he sought
and sent the summons by his dearest wife,
his spouse who sends her, and the joy and pride
of the fond Father to whose realm she hied.

102

‚ÄúEnter‚Äôd Maria, fairest of the fair,
her Father‚Äôs palace-halls of tow‚Äôering height;
lovely her gest though joy was crusht by care
that brimmed her beauteous eyes with tears that blight:
and waved her glorious wealth of golden hair
o‚Äôer neck and shoulders iv‚Äôory-smooth and white:
Before her gladly-greeting Sire she stood,
and told her mission in this melting mood:‚ÅÝ‚Äî

103

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòWhatever various races Earth hath borne,75
the fierce strange peoples of all Africk-land
leadeth Marocco‚Äôs mighty Monarch, sworn
our noble Spain to conquer and command:
Power like this ne‚Äôer met beneath the Morn
since bitter Ocean learnt to bathe the strand:
They bring such fierceness and a rage so dread
the Living shake and quake the buried Dead.

104

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòHe to whose arms thou gavest me to wife,
his land defending when such foes invade,
offers himself, o‚Äôerfeeble for the strife,
to the hard mercies of the Moorish blade;
if, Sire! thou deign not aid that all-dear life,
me shalt thou see from out the kingdom fade,
widow√®d, wretched, doomed to lot obscure,
sans realm, sans husband, e‚Äôen sans life secure.

105

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòWherefore, O King! of whom for purest fear,
Mulucha‚Äôs76 currents in their course congeal;
cast from thee dull delay, rise, swift appear
a second Saviour to our sad Castile:
If this thy count‚Äôenance, beaming love so dear,
set on a Father‚Äôs fond proud heart its seal,
haste, Father! succour, an thou hasten not,
haply he faileth who thy succour sought.‚Äô

106

‚ÄúNot otherwise fear-filled Maria spake
her Sire, than Venus when, in saddest strain,
she pled to great All-Father for the sake
of her Aeneas tossing on the Main;
and in Jove‚Äôs breast could such compassion ‚Äôwake,
his dreadful thunders from his hand fall vain:
The clement Godhead all to her concedeth
and mourneth only that no more she needeth.

107

‚ÄúBut now the squadded warriors muster dense
on Eborensian plains with fierce array;
glint in the sun-glare harness, sword, spear, lance,
and richly furnisht destriers prance and neigh:
The banner‚Äôd trumpets with a blast advance,
rousing men‚Äôs bosoms from the gentle sway
of holy Peace to dire refulgent arms,
and down the dales reverb‚Äôerate War‚Äôs alarms.77

108

‚ÄúMajestic marcheth, girt by all his powers,
th‚Äô insignia of his Royal state among,
valiant Afonso, and his tall form towers
by neck and shoulders taller than the throng;
his gest alone embraves the heart that cowers,
in his stout presence wax the weaklings strong:
Thus to Castilia‚Äôs realm he leads his band,
with his fair daughter, Ladye of the Land.

109

‚ÄúIn fine when met the Kings, Afonsos twain,78
upon Tarifa‚Äôs field, they stand to front
that swarming host of stone-blind heathen men,
for whom are small the meadows and the mount.
No sprite there liveth of so tough a grain,
but feels its faith and trust of small account,
did it not clearly see and fully know,
Christ by His servants‚Äô arms shall smite the foe.

110

‚ÄúThe seed of Hagar laughing, as it were,
to view the Christian pow‚Äôer so weak, so mean;
begins the lands, as though their own, to share
ere won, among the conqu‚Äôering Hagarene;
such forg√®d title and false style they bear
claiming the famous name of Saracene:
Thus with false reck‚Äôoning would they strip and spoil
calling it theirs, that noble alien soil.

111

‚ÄúE‚Äôen so the barb‚Äôarous Giant huge and gaunt,
with cause to royal Saul so dread appearing
when seen the swordless Shepherd stand afront,
armed but with pebbles and with heart unfearing;
launch√®d his sneer of pride and arr‚Äôogant taunt
at the weak youngling‚Äôs humble raiment jeering,
who, whirled the sling, soon read the lesson well,
how much shall Faith all human force excel:

112

‚ÄúThus do the Moormen, traitor-souls, despise
our Christian forces, nor can understand
how Heav‚Äôen‚Äôs high fortress wonted aid affies,
which e‚Äôen horrific Hell may not withstand:
On this and on his skill Castile relies,
falls on Marocco‚Äôs King, strikes hand to hand:
The Portingall, who holds all danger light,
makes the Gran√°dan kingdom fear his might.

113

‚ÄúBehold! the brandisht blade and lance at rest,
rang loud on coat and crest, a wild ons√®t!
They cried, as each his several law confest,
these ‚ÄòSanct‚Äô Thiago!‚Äô and those ‚ÄòMuhammad!‚Äô
The cries of wounded men the skies opprest,
whose flowing blood in ugly puddles met,
where other half-slain wretches drowning lay,
who dragged their shatter‚Äôd limbs from out the fray.

114

‚ÄúWith such prevailing force the Lusian fought
the Gr√°nadil, that in the shortest space
an utter ruin of his host was wrought;
ne fence, ne steely plate our strokes could face:
With such triumphant Vict‚Äôory cheaply bought
unsatisfied, the Strong Arm79 flies apace,
and timely aids Castilia‚Äôs toiling pow‚Äôer,
still mixt in doubtful conflict with the Moor.

115

‚ÄúNow brightly burning Sol had housed his wain
in Thetis‚Äô bower, and his slanting ray
sank westward, bearing Hesper in his train,
to close that rare and most memorious day:
When of the Moors those valiant Sovrans twain
the dense and dreadful squadrons swept away,
with such fell slaughter as ne‚Äôer told of Man
the page of Story since the world began.

116

‚ÄúNe‚Äôer could strong Marius e‚Äôen the quarter show,
of lives here victim‚Äôd by victorious Fate;
when to the river, red with gory glow,
he sent his thirsty Braves their drouth to sate:
Ne yet the Carthaginian, asp‚Äôerous foe
to Roman pow‚Äôer and cradled in her hate,
when slain so many Knights of noble Rome,
of their gold rings he sent three bushels home.

117

‚ÄúAnd if sole thou so many souls to flit
couldst force, and seek Cocytus‚Äô reign of night,
when thou the Holy City didst acquit
of the base J√∫dean, firm in olden rite;
‚Äôtwas that Jehovah‚Äôs vengeance thus saw fit,
O noble Titus! not thine arm of might;
for thus inspir√®d men had prophesied,
and thus by Jesu‚Äôs lips ‚Äôtwas certified.

118

‚ÄúAccomplish√®d his act of arms victorious,
home to his Lusian realm Afonso sped,
to gain from Peacetide triumphs great and glorious,
as those he gained in wars and battles dread;80
when the sad chance, on History‚Äôs page memorious,
which can unsepulchre the sheeted dead,
befell that ill-starr‚Äôd miserable Dame
who, foully slain, a thron√®d Queen became.

119

‚ÄúThou, only thou, pure Love, whose cruel might
obligeth human hearts to weal and woe,
thou, only thou, didst wreak such foul despight,
as though she were some foul perfidious foe.
Thy burning thirst, fierce Love, they say aright,
may not be quencht by saddest tears that flow;
nay, more, thy sprite of harsh tyrannick mood
would see thine altars bathed with human blood.

120

‚ÄúHe placed thee, fair Ign√®z! in soft retreat,
culling the first-fruits of thy sweet young years,
in that delicious Dream, that dear Deceit,
whose long endurance Fortune hates and fears:
Hard by Mondego‚Äôs yearned-for meads thy seat,
where linger, flowing still, those lovely tears,
until each hill-born tree and shrub confest
the name of Him deep writ within thy breast.81

121

‚ÄúThere, in thy Prince awoke responsive-wise
dear thoughts of thee which soul-deep ever lay;
which brought thy beauteous form before his eyes,
whene‚Äôer those eyne of thine were far away:
Night fled in falsest, sweetest fantasies,
in fleeting, flying reveries sped the Day;
and all, in fine, he saw or cared to see
were memories of his love, his joys, his thee.

122

‚ÄúOf many a dainty dame and damosel
the coveted nuptial couches he rejecteth;
for nought can e‚Äôer, pure Love! thy care dispel,
when one enchanting shape thy heart subjecteth.
These whims of passion to despair compel
the Sire, whose old man‚Äôs wisdom aye respecteth,
his subjects murmuring at his son‚Äôs delay
to bless the nation with a bridal day.

123

‚ÄúTo wrench Ign√®z from life he doth design,
better his captured son from her to wrench;
deeming that only blood of death indign
the living lowe of such true Love can quench.
What Fury willed it that the steel so fine,
which from the mighty weight would never flinch
of the dread Moorman, should be drawn in hate
to work that hapless delicate Ladye‚Äôs fate?

124

‚ÄúThe horr‚Äôible Hangmen hurried her before
the King, now moved to spare her innocence;
but still her cruel murther urged the more,
the People swayed by fierce and false pretence.
She with her pleadings pitiful and sore,
that told her sorrows and her care immense
for her Prince-spouse and babes, whom more to leave
than her own death the mother‚Äôs heart did grieve:

125

‚ÄúAnd heav‚Äôenwards to the clear and chryst‚Äôalline skies,
raising her eyne with piteous tears bestain√®d;
her eyne, because her hands with cruel ties
one of the wicked Ministers82 constrain√®d:
And gazing on her babes in wistful guise,
whose pretty forms she loved with love unfeign√®d,
whose orphan‚Äôd lot the Mother filled with dread,
unto their cruel grandsire thus she said‚ÅÝ‚Äî

126

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòIf the brute-creatures, which from natal day
on cruel ways by Nature‚Äôs will were bent;
or feral birds whose only thought is prey,
